label: fiction
title: The Sleepwalker's Kitchen
charge: Wounding with intent to cause grievous bodily harm
elements: The accused struck the injured man.
He intended to cause him serious harm.

beats.text: The injured brother-in-law describes it: at three in the morning he woke on the sofa-bed to find the accused standing over him with the pan, eyes wide open. 'He looked straight at me. He knew exactly what he was doing.'
beats.reveal_note: The detail that convinces everyone proves the opposite of what it seems: open, unseeing eyes are the textbook presentation of the condition.

beats.text: The defence produces a sleep clinic's file opened nineteen years earlier: recorded episodes, an overnight EEG study, and a documented history of complex behaviour while asleep — including cooking full meals.
beats.reveal_note: The diagnosis predates the marriage, the quarrel, and any imaginable motive by two decades. Nobody plants a medical history nineteen years deep.

beats.text: The accused's own sister concedes there had been words at dinner — about money the couple had lent the visitor and not seen back.
beats.reveal_note: A mild, resolved dinner-table disagreement — retold in a courtroom, it swells into motive. Every family has that evening; almost none has this night.

beats.text: The emergency call is played: seconds after 'waking', the accused's voice — confusion, then horror, begging the operator to hurry.
beats.reveal_note: Real distress, seconds old. It corroborates the medicine but couldn't stand alone — which is why the clinic file matters more.

beats.text: The judge: the charge requires intent, and intent requires a conscious mind. An act done while genuinely asleep is not, in law, the act of a guilty man.
beats.reveal_note: The whole case in one instruction: the question was never who held the pan, but whether anyone was behind the eyes.

twist: He did it — and he was asleep. The open eyes that terrified the victim are the classic sign of the condition, and the sleep-clinic file is nineteen years older than the quarrel. The law asks for a guilty mind; that night there was no mind present at all.
